specialties.what_it_is: Distinct from AFSC 2A6X1 (Aerospace Propulsion, jet-engine maintenance) — data/interest-taxonomy.json's mechanical-engineering cluster example list names 2A6X1, but this run's binding constraint specifically named 2A6X2 (Aerospace Ground Equipment). specialties.name: Air Traffic Control
specialties.what_it_is: UNIQUE TO THIS AFSC IN THE AVIATION CLUSTER: an Air Force air traffic controller must earn and hold an actual FAA Air Traffic Control Specialist Certificate before being allowed to independently perform certified controller duties — per bestmilitaryresume.com, 'the candidate must be able to meet the testing and other requirements to be awarded a Federal Aviation Administration Specialist Certificate' and 'any Air Force enlisted member aspiring to be an air traffic controller must possess this certificate before being allowed to assume the duties of a certified Air Force Air Traffic Controller.' This is earned during initial technical training/certification, not automatically granted at enlistment — but unlike the aircraft-maintenance credential gap in this cluster, a fully qualified 1C1X1 controller genuinely does hold a real FAA credential by the end of qualification. UNVERIFIED at primary-source level this session (search-summarized, not independently confirmed against a primary FAA/Air Force document).

specialties.name: All Source Intelligence Analyst
specialties.what_it_is: Under DoD's 13 Adjudicative Guidelines (Security Executive Agent Directive 4 / 32 CFR Part 147), none of the following are automatic mechanical disqualifiers under the 'whole person' standard, but they are the dominant real-world reasons applicants and incumbents are denied or lose eligibility, and each can rule out this specialty even with a fully qualifying ASVAB score: (1) unresolved foreign contacts, foreign relatives in certain countries, foreign property/financial interests, or foreign romantic ties that create potential foreign leverage (Guideline B, Foreign Influence); (2) a pattern of serious financial delinquency -- unresolved debt (especially over $10,000), recent bankruptcy, wage garnishment, or repeated late payment patterns (Guideline F; financial issues are cited as roughly 45% of DoD clearance denials); (3) illegal drug use, misuse of prescription medication, or continued use after applying (Guideline H); and (4), treated especially seriously, deliberately concealing or falsifying any of the above -- including an UNREPORTED dual citizenship or undisclosed foreign contact -- during the application or investigation (Guideline E, Personal Conduct). A 16-24-year-old considering this cluster should treat clearance eligibility as a real, independent gate, not a formality layered on top of a qualifying ASVAB score.

entry_requirements.security_clearance: TS/SCI required, or must be submitted for TS/SCI prior to course attendance -- CONFIRMED this session via search. Completion of a current Tier 5 (T5) investigation (per DoDM 5200.02, AFMAN 16-1405) is mandatory; an Airman who cannot obtain at least an interim T5 by the programmed class-start date is not eligible to enter the AFSC.

Recommend the taxonomy file be corrected from 3F0X2 to 3F1X1 in a future pass; flagged here rather than silently overridden.

specialties.name: Space Systems Operations Specialist
specialties.what_it_is: U.S. citizenship required. IMPORTANT STRUCTURAL NOTE: airforce.com (fetched 2026-07-12) lists this AFSC as open across Air Force Active Duty, Air Force Reserve, and Air National Guard — it does NOT state that the mission or accession pipeline transferred to the Space Force, even though technical training occurs at Vandenberg Space Force Base (the same location used for Space Force's own UST). This is a genuinely surprising finding worth flagging rather than silently reconciling: the most defensible read, consistent with public reporting that the Space Force has no reserve/Guard component of its own, is that active-duty 1C6X1 space-operations manning has largely transitioned to the Space Force (its Space Systems Operator career field, previous record in this file) while Air National Guard and Air Force Reserve units performing space-surveillance/missile-warning missions (which the Space Force cannot yet absorb, having no Guard/Reserve of its own) continue to access and train personnel under the legacy Air Force AFSC 1C6X1. This interpretation is NOT independently confirmed against a primary source this session and is offered as the most defensible reading of directly-observed, conflicting official material, not as a verified fact.
